danger_signals.visual_form: Enemy appearance on world map during raft travel
danger_signals.meaning: Transition from safe world map to hostile side-scrolling combat perspective

danger_signals.visual_form: Bird, fox, boar, lizard, dog, crab enemies with distinct sprites
danger_signals.meaning: Indicates stage danger level and type of threat player must overcome

danger_signals.visual_form: Locked camera positioning, extended room design (Mammoth boss chamber extends right, tree boss chamber extends upward)
danger_signals.meaning: Spatially constrains player movement, signals climactic danger zone

danger_signals.visual_form: Obscured floor layer beneath foreground grey stone tiles
danger_signals.meaning: Environmental hazard layering suggests danger below visible surface

safety_signals.visual_form: Indoor tilemaps with opaque background layer, sky never visible
safety_signals.meaning: Safe hub area where player recovers and receives quest information

safety_signals.visual_form: Tina's face sprite appears in Inn map overlay during boss completion
safety_signals.meaning: Narrative confirmation of progress and safety checkpoint reached

safety_signals.visual_form: Overworld navigation perspective with random encounter possibility
safety_signals.meaning: Semi-safe exploration state with controlled threat (random rather than constant)

faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Inn and King's chambers as central hub locations, formal interior architecture

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Player character sprite with amnesia-driven quest motivation
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Travels by raft, explores islands, gains equipment and items for progression

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Bird, fox, boar, lizard, dog, crab enemy sprites
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Island-specific enemy distributions across Waku-Waku, Poka-Poka, Hiya-Hiya islands

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Master Higgins wakes on unknown island with no memory; story setup occurs in Inn/King's chambers interior tileset
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Player must explore to recover lost memories; NPCs direct player toward kidnapped queen rescue mission

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: World map divided into separate islands (Waku-Waku, Poka-Poka, Hiya-Hiya with distinct visual palettes); raft-based navigation
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Player travels between islands collecting items and equipment; random encounters trigger perspective shift to side-scrolling combat

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Player warped to Inn, Tina's face appears in cutscene overlay, then warped back to completed stage exterior
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Quest progress tracked via visual narrative; new areas become accessible based on equipment acquired

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Inventory system reflects weapons, armor, shields, spells, and equipment pieces; visual equipment changes on player sprite
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: New equipment enables access to previously blocked areas (Metroidvania-style gating); progression non-linear across multiple islands

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Gameplay perspective shifts from world map overhead to side-scrolling view; enemy spawn with varied sprite types
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Player must defeat enemies or flee; encounter resolution returns player to world map state

environmental_storytelling.example: Inn map uses opaque first background layer with no transparency, hiding unfinished sky layer beneath; same map reused for King's chambers and post-boss cutscenes, communicating location as narrative anchor

Boss chambers contain camera-locked regions that extend beyond visible play area (Mammoth boss chamber extends right, tree boss chamber extends upward with extended foliage); hints at world scale beyond immediate interaction

Waku-Waku Island, Poka-Poka Island, Hiya-Hiya Island feature distinct enemy palettes and environmental palettes; same miniboss map reused but with different color schemes to indicate location identity
Miniboss cavern floor obscured by foreground grey stone tiles; implies environmental hazards and obstacles exist beneath visible surface, creating depth of spatial danger
Tina's face sprite embedded in Inn background layer, revealed only when background layer toggled off; communicates emotional stakes of amnesia plot and recovery theme

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Appears as common monster type; sprite animations include running and jumping; suggests airborne threat
Common monster type loaded across multiple islands; sprite suggests terrestrial swift threat
Sprite loaded into VRAM on Poka-Poka, Hiya-Hiya, Puka-Puka islands and random encounters; complete sprite animations exist but enemy never placed in actual maps; suggests development iteration cut
Shares miniboss cavern map with dog miniboss; chamber has obscured floor layer; behavior tied to specific stage completion trigger
Shares miniboss cavern map with lizard; palette swap indicates location identity despite identical behavioral map
Occupies Poka-Poka Island boss chamber; camera lock prevents rightward scrolling but extended chamber exists beyond; defeat triggers stage completion narrative
Occupies Hiya-Hiya Island boss chamber; camera lock prevents upward scrolling but extended foliage chamber exists above; defeat triggers stage completion narrative
Listed as Stage 6 boss in debug menu; represents late-game threat tier
Listed as final boss in debug menu; represents ultimate world-state threat, story climax
Quest-driven exploration; seeks queen rescue while recovering memories; equipment acquisition unlocks new pathways; raft travel indicates semi-vulnerable state
Also suffers amnesia; appointed by king; kidnapping drives main narrative; recovery of her location serves as story goal
Resides in King's chambers; requests Higgins undertake queen rescue; provides quest motivation; represents political stability seeking

notes: Super Adventure Island II communicates world-state primarily through perspective shifts (world map raft travel vs. side-scrolling stages vs. interior hub), enemy sprite variation across islands, and equipment-gated progression typical of Metroidvania design. Danger signals rely heavily on enemy type presence and boss chamber architecture. Safety is communicated through interior tileset opacity and hub location design. Political situation (king seeking restoration of kidnapped queen) is subtle, embedded in dialogue-driven narrative rather than visual manifestation. Environmental storytelling emphasizes layered map design with hidden extensions and sprite embedding to create narrative depth. The game's core manifestation mechanic is the conversion of abstract world-state (amnesia, quest progress, equipment acquired) into visible equipment on player sprite and accessible map areas.
sources_used: tcrf - Super Adventure Island II (Cutting Room Floor wiki)
